summary: Yegge argues that an alarming number of professional programmers cannot touch-type, and this is the software industry's 'dirtiest little secret.' Through a humorous autobiographical story about learning to type from a football coach in 1982, he explains the Fast-Slow-Medium practice method borrowed from classical musicians. He contends that non-touch-typists make visible sacrifices: minimal code comments, poor formatting, sparse online participation, and weak design communication. The post is an impassioned plea for every programmer to invest the modest time required to learn proper typing, arguing there are simply no valid excuses.
key_points: Non-touch-typist programmers sacrifice documentation, code comments, formatting, and online community participation to compensate for their slow input speed
The Fast-Slow-Medium practice method from classical music training applies directly to learning typing: speed first, then accuracy, then musical/natural flow
Learning to touch-type requires only about 30 days of 30-minute daily practice with modern software — a trivial investment for a career-defining skill
Refactoring tools and IDEs are not substitutes for typing ability — claiming otherwise is like saying you can saw your legs off because you have a car
Design work requires persistent written communication with other people, so the claim that 'I spend all my time designing, not typing' actually argues for typing skill, not against it
money_quotes: If you're not typing as part of your design, then you're not doing design right.
If you really think refactoring tools are a substitute for typing, it's like you're telling us that it's OK for you to saw your legs off because you have a car.
It's empowering, being able to type almost as fast as you can think. Why would you want it any other way?
Non-touch-typists have to make sacrifices in order to sustain their productivity.
I have a friend, John, who can only use one of his hands. He types 70 wpm. He invented his own technique for it. He's not making excuses; he's typing circles around people who are making excuses.
Let's face it: it's lazy. There's just no excuse for it.
It's like being, I dunno, an actor without knowing how to put your clothes on. It's showing up to the game unprepared.
key_insight: Touch-typing is such a fundamental programming skill that not learning it forces programmers to silently sacrifice code quality, documentation, and collaborative design participation — yet the industry politely ignores this widespread deficiency.
